data.title: Guzman y Gomez shares plunge as US sales disappoint
data.description:
By Nichiket Sunil, Sameer Manekar and Kumar Tanishk

Feb 20 (Reuters) - Mexican fast-food chain Guzman y Gomez's ( GYGLF ) ASX-listed shares slumped to a record low on Friday, as investors ignored a half-yearly profit beat for disappointing U.S. sales.

GYG shares ‚Äãclosed down nearly 14% at A$17.53, making it ‚Äåone of the biggest drags on the benchmark S&P/ASX 200 index, which was 0.1% down. It had ‚ÅÝearlier slumped as much as 16.5% to A$17.¬Ý

The firm reported net profit ‚ÅÝafter tax of A$10.6 million ($7.46 million), higher than last year's ‚ÄåA$7.3 million and ‚Äåbeating the Visible Alpha consensus of A$9.2 million. The company expects U.S. losses to increase slightly in ‚Äãthe year to June from A$13.2 million ‚Äålast year.

Hayden Beamish, portfolio manager at Endeavor Asset Management, called the earnings beat "noise".

The company's appeal to investors has rested on its ‚Äãpotential to crack the U.S. fast-casual ‚Äãmarket. It ‚Äåwas a central part of its 2024 IPO pitch, which priced the stock at A$22 with a valuation of A$3 billion, leaving the ‚ÅÝstock vulnerable when its American momentum falters.¬Ý

GYG's valuation has always depended on ‚ÅÝa successful U.S. expansion, and without clear progress there, investors feel they're paying a growth multiple for what is essentially an Australian fast-food chain, said Beamish.

GYG also flagged short-term U.S. sales momentum pressure after it shifted to Uber ‚ÅÝEats from ‚ÄåDoorDash.

U.S. sales jumped 67% to A$8.2 million ($5.77 million) in ‚Äåthe first half, below Visible Alpha's consensus of A$9.2 million. Inclement weather in the ‚ÅÝgreater Chicago area in the December quarter also hurt comparable sales growth.

Considered a gauge of sentiment toward Australia's quick-service restaurant sector, the company's local segment, its biggest contributor to sales, reported a 17.5% sales increase to A$673.6 million, and forecast full-year profit margins of up to 6.2% from 5.7% last year.

Group network sales jumped 18% to A$681.8 ‚Äãmillion, missing the Visible Alpha consensus of A$687.3 million.

The company declared an interim dividend of 7.4 Australian cents per share.

($1 = 1.4209 Australian dollars)

(Reporting by Sameer Manekar ‚Äãand Nichiket Sunil in Bengaluru, ‚Äåadditional reporting by Kumar Tanishk; Editing by Jonathan Ananda, ‚ÄãMaju Samuel and Harikrishnan Nair)

data.title: Exclusive-Netflix has ample room to increase its offer in battle for Warner Bros, sources say
data.description:
Feb 19 (Reuters) - Netflix ( NFLX ) has ample cash and could bump up its offer for HBO Max owner Warner Bros Discovery ( WBD ) if competing bidder Paramount Skydance ( PSKY ) increases its own offer, two people with knowledge of the matter said.¬Ý

The two media giants have been locked in a heated rivalry over Warner Bros and its storied catalogue, which includes iconic franchises ‚Äãlike "Harry Potter", "Game of Thrones", DC Comics and Superman.¬Ý

Though Warner Bros is moving ‚Äåforward with a March 20 shareholder vote on Netflix's ( NFLX ) offer, it has given Paramount a week to come up with a more compelling bid.

Netflix ( NFLX ) has bid $27.75 a share, or $82.7 billion, ‚ÅÝfor Warner Bros' studio and streaming businesses while Paramount has offered $30 a share, or $108.4 billion, for the whole company, which includes ‚ÅÝDiscovery Global that houses CNN, HGTV and other TV assets.¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý

Netflix ( NFLX ) and Warner Bros declined to comment.¬Ý

The ‚Äåcreator of "Stranger Things" is sitting on ‚Äåa lot of dry powder that gives it some flexibility to up the ante, the people said, holding about $9.03 billion in cash and cash equivalents on its balance ‚Äãsheet as of December 31.

MONDAY DEADLINE

Warner Bros rejected Paramount's latest hostile takeover ‚Äåbid on Tuesday but gave the rival studio until the end of Monday to submit a "best and final" offer. Paramount enticed the board to the table after informally broaching a $31 per share offer, Warner Bros said.¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý

"Netflix ( NFLX ) ‚Äãstill looks to be in the driving seat, but that can ‚Äãquickly shift," said ‚ÄåMatt Britzman, senior equity analyst at Hargreaves Lansdown. "Price will likely be the deciding factor - Warner's concerns around funding and regulatory risk are real, but at a high enough number, they become secondary."

Britzman expects Netflix ( NFLX ) will counter any improved offer ‚ÅÝfrom Paramount. "But the real twist is that these deals were never apples-to-apples, and it may ultimately come down to how ‚ÅÝmuch value the board and shareholders assign to the network business that Netflix ( NFLX ) would leave behind," he said.

Paramount said it would continue to push the tender offer it has launched for the studio, oppose the "inferior" Netflix ( NFLX ) merger and still plans to nominate directors for the upcoming Warner Bros annual meeting.

All eyes are now on whether the CBS-parent improves its offer, which Netflix ( NFLX ) is allowed to match under the terms ‚ÅÝof the ‚Äåmerger agreement, according to Warner Bros.¬Ý

Warner Bros Chairman Samuel DiPiazza Jr. and CEO David Zaslav said ‚Äåin a letter sent to the Paramount board on Tuesday that "we continue to recommend and remain fully committed to our transaction with Netflix ( NFLX )".

BOARD ‚ÅÝCONCERNS

Paren Knadjian, partner at Eisner Advisory Group, said Paramount's persistence suggests it thinks it can win.

"Board-level concerns around financing structure, timing and regulatory approval meaningfully detract from the attractiveness of Paramount's proposal, irrespective of headline valuation," he said.

Paramount last week proposed paying Warner Bros investors additional cash for every quarter the deal does not close after this year, and said it would cover the $2.8 billion breakup fee that Warner Bros would owe Netflix ( NFLX ) if it withdrew from their agreement. But that was not enough for Warner Bros, which said the revised terms still did not meet the threshold for what ‚Äãits board would deem a superior proposal.

In a letter, the board said Paramount's offer left several issues unresolved, including responsibility for a potential $1.5 billion junior lien financing fee, how the transaction would proceed if debt financing fell through, and whether equity funding led by Larry ‚ÄãEllison was fully committed.¬Ý

data.title: Research Alert: CFRA Keeps Buy Opinion On Shares Of Firstenergy Corp.
data.description:
12:40 AM EST, 02/20/2026 (MT Newswires) -- CFRA, an independent research provider, has provided MT Newswires with the following research alert. Analysts at CFRA have summarized their opinion as follows:

Our 12-month target price of $56 (up $2) reflects a 19x multiple applied to our 2027 EPS estimate. Our target multiple is near peers to reflect competitive near-term EPS and dividend growth expectations. We raise our 2026 EPS estimate by $0.03 to $2.74 and start 2027's at $2.94. Though electric sales growth remains modest, FE expects industrial customer loads to support sales growth in 2026. We think FE's improving earned ROE, recent capex guidance increase (+7% for 2026 to $6.0B and expectations for ~30% transmission investment boost from 2026-2030 vs. 2025-2029), and attractive data center load pipeline in its service territory lend support to the company's 6%-8% EPS CAGR target from 2026-2030. Shares yield 3.75% based on projected 2026 dividends of $1.86 per share. A rate base case in Ohio (for the period of July 2027 to June 2030) is likely to be filed in the early second quarter of 2026.

MT Newswires does not provide investment advice. Unauthorized reproduction is strictly prohibited.

data.title: Trump threatens Iran with force
data.description:
A look at the day ahead in European and global markets from Tom Westbrook

Traders were hunkering down into the weekend, with threats flying between the U.S. and Iran, and nerves rippling through ‚Äãmarkets from listed private equity funds.

PMI surveys, ‚ÄåU.S. PCE and GDP data and a smattering of corporate earnings headline the calendar, but politics ‚ÅÝand the risk of war seemed to loom largest in investors' ‚ÅÝminds as the dollar headed for its largest ‚Äåweekly rise in four ‚Äåmonths.

U.S. President Donald Trump has set a deadline of 10 to 15 days for Iran ‚Äãto cut a deal over its ‚Äåpursuit of nuclear weapons, or face "really bad things".

It is classic Trump theatre, so asset prices have not yet moved ‚Äãa great deal. But the U.S. ‚Äãhas sent ‚Äåaircraft carriers, warships and jets to the region and traders are poised to send oil and the dollar higher in the ‚ÅÝevent of conflict.

Iran said it would respond if attacked.

Brent crude futures ‚ÅÝhave topped $72 a barrel - a 6-1/2 month high - and the dollar was firm in the Asia session.

Trump, meanwhile, posted about aliens on social media, saying he would order Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth and other agencies ‚ÅÝto ‚Äårelease government files related to aliens and unidentified flying ‚Äåobjects.

It makes you wonder what he is trying to distract from.

Walmart's new ‚ÅÝboss sounded cautious on consumer spending on Thursday.¬Ý

And private equity stocks hit the skids after Blue Owl's latest strategy to return capital from a small debt fund spooked investors.

Blue Owl said it sold $1.4 billion in assets, to return some of the proceeds to investors, and permanently removed an option for quarterly redemptions ‚Äãat one fund - playing into already swirling concerns about valuations and liquidity at PE funds.

Key developments that could influence markets on Friday:

- U.S. ‚ÄãPCE and GDP data

- Global PMI ‚Äåsurveys

- Earnings at Danone, Air Liquide and Anglo ‚ÄãAmerican

(Editing by Kate Mayberry)

data.title: PRECIOUS-Gold heads for weekly loss as dollar advances
data.description:
*

Gold set for weekly decline of about 1%

*

Dollar heads for best week in four months

*

US PCE data due at 1330 GMT

(Updates prices for Asia mid-morning session trade)

By Pablo Sinha

Feb 20 (Reuters) - Gold steadied on Friday, but ‚Äãwas set for a weekly decline as the dollar ‚Äåclimbed to a near one-month high, while investors awaited key U.S. inflation data to assess the Federal Reserve's ‚ÅÝmonetary policy moving forward.

Spot gold was steady at $5,000.40 per ounce, as of 0530 ‚ÅÝGMT, and was down about 1% for the week ‚Äåso far. U.S. ‚Äågold futures for April delivery were up 0.4% at $5,019.10.

"Precious metals are consolidating with a slight downward bias at ‚Äãthis time... We've seen the dollar picking ‚Äåup from its lows and that led to a bit of a pressure in precious metals," said GoldSilver Central Managing Director Brian ‚ÄãLan.

"Even during this period when there ‚Äãis no ‚ÄåChina market to support gold, we've seen that prices have more or less been steady, which also tells you that there is still a ‚ÅÝlot of buying in lower levels for gold."

Markets in Mainland China and Taiwan ‚ÅÝwere closed for the Lunar New Year holidays.

The dollar was set for its strongest weekly performance since October, supported by a run of stronger-than-expected economic data, a more hawkish Federal Reserve outlook and lingering tensions between the U.S. and Iran.

The Personal ‚ÅÝConsumption ‚ÄåExpenditure (PCE) data, the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, for December is now ‚Äåin focus for clues on U.S. monetary policy.

Markets currently expect the Fed to ‚ÅÝdeliver its first rate of the year in June, according to CME's FedWatch Tool.

Non-yielding bullion tends to do well in low-interest-rate environments.

Goldman Sachs said in a note that under the base case scenario, it expects central bank buying to re-accelerate, while private investors will add exposure only in response to Fed rate cuts, driving gold higher to $5,400/troy ounce by end-2026.

It ‚Äãalso said it continues to see the medium-term trajectory for gold prices as upward, potentially with elevated volatility.

Elsewhere, spot silver edged 0.2% higher to $78.47 per ounce. Spot ‚Äãplatinum ticked up 0.1% to $2,071.63 per ounce, ‚Äåwhile palladium gained 0.1% to $1,684.59. (Reporting by Pablo Sinha in ‚ÄãBengaluru; Editing by Subhranshu Sahu)

data.title: NASA report recalls dysfunction, heated emotions during Boeing's botched Starliner flight
data.description:
WASHINGTON, Feb 19 (Reuters) - NASA on Thursday released a sweeping report on Boeing's ( BA ) botched Starliner mission that kept two astronauts stuck on the International Space Station for nine months, detailing communication breakdowns and "unprofessional behavior" as the agency and its longtime contractor struggled to agree on how to safely return the crew to Earth.

NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman ripped into Boeing ( BA ) and agency leadership for their handling of the Starliner mission during a news conference timed with the release of a 300-page report detailing technical and oversight failures behind the spacecraft's first crewed mission, ‚Äãwhich concluded last year.

"Starliner has design and engineering deficiencies that must be corrected, but the most troubling failure ‚Äårevealed by this investigation is not hardware," Isaacman wrote in a letter to NASA employees, which he posted in full on X.

"It is decision making and leadership that, if left unchecked, could create a culture incompatible with human spaceflight," he added, echoing findings in the ‚ÅÝreport's "cultural and organizational" section.

Starliner's technical failures kept NASA astronauts Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams on the ISS for nine months in a high-stakes test mission initially planned to last roughly a week.

On ‚ÅÝEarth, according to the report, Boeing ( BA ) and NASA officials sparred in tense meetings on how best to bring the crew home, with "unprofessional behavior" ‚Äåand yelling matches that countered the agency's norms ‚Äåof healthy technical debate and crisis management.

The report, completed in November and citing interviews with unnamed NASA officials, said "numerous interviewees mentioned defensive, unhealthy, contentious meetings during technical disagreements early in the mission."

"There was yelling in meetings. It was emotionally charged and unproductive," one ‚Äãofficial reported. "It was probably the ugliest environment that I've been in," another said.

"There wasn't a clear path ‚Äåfor conflict resolution between the teams. That led to a lot of frayed relationships and emotions," said another.

Boeing ( BA ) said in a statement that it was "grateful to NASA for its thorough investigation and the opportunity to contribute to it." The company, it added, has made progress on fixing Starliner's technical issues and has made organizational ‚Äãchanges.

"WE FAILED THEM"

Wilmore and Williams, both veteran test pilots and astronauts, launched as Starliner's first test ‚Äãcrew in June 2024. Five ‚Äåof the spacecraft's maneuvering thrusters failed roughly 24 hours into flight as it was approaching the ISS for an autonomous docking, prompting the crew to manually intervene.

The thruster issues were among four primary technical flaws Starliner experienced during the mission that set off months of debate and ground tests as "Butch and Suni" stayed on the ISS. They ‚ÅÝreturned to Earth last year on a SpaceX craft after NASA opted to return Starliner to Earth empty.

"They have so much grace, and they're so competent, the two of them. ‚ÅÝAnd we failed them. The agency failed them," NASA Associate Administrator Amit Kshatriya told reporters.

Williams, now 60, retired from NASA in December, logging 608 days in space across three missions in her 27-year NASA career. Wilmore, now 63, retired in August after spending 25 years at the agency, clocking 464 days in space across three missions.

The report also describes a "fragile partnership dynamic" between NASA and Boeing ( BA ), in which agency officials' concerns that Boeing ( BA ) could drop out of NASA's Commercial Crew Program influenced officials' decision-making on critical mission issues.¬Ý

"This reluctance to challenge Boeing's ( BA ) interpretations and failure to act on engineering concerns has contributed to ‚ÅÝrisk acceptance and a ‚Äåfragile partnership dynamic."

NASA retroactively classified the Starliner mission as a "Type A" mishap, the agency's most severe category of mission failure, triggered by factors ‚Äåsuch as damage to a spacecraft exceeding $2 million or a crew member's death or permanent disability.

Boeing ( BA ) has spent tens of millions of dollars on efforts to fix Starliner following the mission,. The company has taken ‚ÅÝroughly $2 billion in charges so far on the program since 2016.

But NASA last year reduced the contract's total value to $3.7 billion and cut the number of planned Starliner flights from six to four, as Boeing's ( BA ) engineering struggles inch closer to 2030, the planned retirement of the ISS.

RARE LEVEL OF DISCLOSURE FROM NASA'S COMMERCIAL CREW PROGRAM

NASA's decision to release a redacted version of its investigative findings was praised by former NASA officials and astronauts and marked a rare move for an agency office that has often sought to portray its collaboration with Boeing's ( BA ) Starliner unit as positive and constructive.

"It isn't easy, but if previous Admins had done same, safety & public trust would be higher," Lori Garver, former Deputy NASA Administrator and a key architect of the agency's commercial-focused contracting model, said of Isaacman's decision to release the report.

NASA's Commercial Crew Program seeded development of Boeing's ( BA ) Starliner and SpaceX's Dragon capsule. ‚ÄãThe agency has made an imperative of having two U.S. vehicles for transporting its astronauts to the ISS in case one encounters issues.

The Dragon capsule has flown over 13 crews for NASA since 2020 with no mission failures, helping position Elon Musk's SpaceX as the U.S. space program's most prominent contractor.¬Ý

Isaacman, a former customer of SpaceX's Dragon program who spent millions of dollars commanding two private missions in ‚Äãorbit, has long been critical of Boeing ( BA ) and other giant government contractors involved in delayed ‚Äåand over-budget programs, a view that has been shared by the Pentagon. Isaacman's ties with Musk concerned lawmakers during his confirmation hearings.

(Reporting by ‚ÄãJoey Roulette; Editing by Chizu Nomiyama, David Gregorio and Diane Craft)

data.title: US economic growth likely slowed to a still-brisk pace in fourth quarter
data.description:
WASHINGTON, Feb 20 (Reuters) - U.S. economic growth likely slowed to a still-solid pace in the fourth quarter because of disruptions from last year's government shutdown and a moderation in consumer spending, though tax cuts and investment in artificial intelligence were expected to drive activity this year.

The anticipated slowdown in gross domestic product would follow back-to-back quarters of robust growth. The Commerce Department will publish on Friday its advance estimate of fourth-quarter GDP, which was delayed by the record 43-day government shutdown.¬Ý

The report is expected to highlight a ‚Äãjobless economic expansion as well as a "K-shaped" economy, where upper-income households are doing well while lower-income consumers ‚Äåare struggling amid high inflation from import tariffs and stalling wage growth. Those conditions have created what economists and President Donald Trump's opponents call an affordability crisis.

"We'll end the year still on a solid note in terms of growth, but it doesn't ‚ÅÝreally translate to feel as good as it looks on paper to most Americans," said Diane Swonk, chief economist at consulting firm KPMG.¬Ý

GDP LIKELY INCREASED 3.0%: SURVEY

GDP probably increased ‚ÅÝat a 3.0% annualized rate last quarter after accelerating at a 4.4% pace in the July-September quarter, a Reuters survey of economists ‚Äåpredicted. The survey was, however, completed before ‚Äådata on Thursday showing the trade deficit widening to a five-month high in December.

The second straight monthly deterioration in the trade deficit led the Atlanta Federal Reserve to cut its GDP estimate to a 3.0% rate from a 3.6% pace.¬Ý

The ‚Äãnonpartisan Congressional Budget Office estimated the government shutdown would subtract 1.5 percentage points from fourth-quarter GDP ‚Äåthrough fewer services provided by federal workers, lower federal spending on goods and services and a temporary reduction in Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program benefits.

The CBO estimated most of the decline in GDP would eventually be recovered, though between $7 billion and $14 billion would not. Economists estimated the economy grew 2.2% in ‚Äã2025 after expanding 2.8% in 2024. Only 181,000 jobs were added last year, the fewest ‚Äãoutside the pandemic since ‚Äåthe 2009 Great Recession, and down from 1.459 million in 2024.¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý

"You have a confluence of shocks affecting the U.S. economy," said Gregory Daco, chief economist at EY-Parthenon. "You have on the one hand the drag from higher prices, tariffs, trade restrictions and reduced immigration, but also the boost from AI investment and the continued ‚ÅÝstrong momentum in terms of stock prices supporting ongoing spending by the more affluent consumers."¬Ý

GROWTH IN CONSUMER SPENDING LIKELY SLOWED

Growth in consumer spending is expected to have slowed ‚ÅÝfrom the third quarter's brisk 3.5% pace. Economists say spending has largely been driven by higher-income households and has been at the expense of saving as inflation eroded buying power.¬Ý

"Getting richer is one thing, but most households rely on incomes to pay bills, and real disposable income pretty much stalled in the quarter," said Sal Guatieri, a senior economist at BMO Capital Markets.

Consumer spending could get a tailwind from what economists anticipate will be larger tax refunds this year because of tax cuts. A solid pace of business investment is expected, mostly related to AI. ‚ÅÝThe jump in imports ‚Äåin December was partly driven by capital goods, mostly computer accessories and telecommunications equipment amid a data center construction boom to ‚Äåpower AI.¬Ý

That should offset any drag on GDP growth from trade.¬Ý

Economists estimated AI, including data centers, semiconductors, software and research and development, accounted for a third of GDP growth in the first ‚ÅÝthree quarters of 2025, blunting the hit from tariffs and reduced immigration.

"It's a significant contribution from a sector that traditionally has represented a small share of the economy," said EY-Parthenon's Daco. "It's also been a key source of volatility in the trade data, because a lot of what we are building here and creating is imported."¬Ý

Economists estimated that trade made little or no contribution to GDP after helping to boost growth for two straight quarters. Inventories were another wild card, having subtracted from GDP for two consecutive quarters.¬Ý

Residential investment is forecast to have contracted for the fourth quarter in a row as builders and prospective homebuyers struggled with higher borrowing costs.¬Ý

The stale report will probably have no impact on monetary policy. But Federal Reserve officials are likely to keep an eye on December's Personal Consumption Expenditures inflation data, due to ‚Äãbe released at the same time as the GDP report.

Economists polled by Reuters forecast PCE inflation, excluding the volatile food and energy components, rising 0.3%. Core PCE inflation rose 0.2% in November from the previous month. Core PCE inflation was projected to have increased 2.9% year-on-year after rising 2.8% in November. The U.S. central bank has a 2% inflation target.

"The year-on-year growth rate ‚Äãof the core has shown essentially no progress since mid-2024," said Lou Crandall, ‚Äåchief economist at Wrightson ICAP. "Many Fed officials anticipate at least some improvement in the coming months, but they will want to ‚Äãsee that show up in the actual numbers."

(Editing by Rod Nickel)

"This reluctance to challenge Boeing's ( BA ) interpretations and failure to act on engineering concerns has contributed to ‚ÅÝrisk acceptance and a ‚Äåfragile partnership dynamic."

NASA retroactively classified the Starliner mission as a "Type A" mishap, the agency's most severe category of mission failure, triggered by factors ‚Äåsuch as damage to a spacecraft exceeding $2 million or a crew member's death or permanent disability.

Boeing ( BA ) has spent tens of millions of dollars on efforts to fix Starliner following the mission,. The company has taken ‚ÅÝroughly $2 billion in charges so far on the program since 2016.

But NASA last year reduced the contract's total value to $3.7 billion and cut the number of planned Starliner flights from six to four, as Boeing's ( BA ) engineering struggles inch closer to 2030, the planned retirement of the ISS.

RARE LEVEL OF DISCLOSURE FROM NASA'S COMMERCIAL CREW PROGRAM

NASA's decision to release a redacted version of its investigative findings was praised by former NASA officials and astronauts and marked a rare move for an agency office that has often sought to portray its collaboration with Boeing's ( BA ) Starliner unit as positive and constructive.

"It isn't easy, but if previous Admins had done same, safety & public trust would be higher," Lori Garver, former Deputy NASA Administrator and a key architect of the agency's commercial-focused contracting model, said of Isaacman's decision to release the report.

NASA's Commercial Crew Program seeded development of Boeing's ( BA ) Starliner and SpaceX's Dragon capsule. ‚ÄãThe agency has made an imperative of having two U.S. vehicles for transporting its astronauts to the ISS in case one encounters issues.

The Dragon capsule has flown over 13 crews for NASA since 2020 with no mission failures, helping position Elon Musk's SpaceX as the U.S. space program's most prominent contractor.¬Ý

Isaacman, a former customer of SpaceX's Dragon program who spent millions of dollars commanding two private missions in ‚Äãorbit, has long been critical of Boeing ( BA ) and other giant government contractors involved in delayed ‚Äåand over-budget programs, a view that has been shared by the Pentagon. Isaacman's ties with Musk concerned lawmakers during his confirmation hearings.

(Reporting by ‚ÄãJoey Roulette; Editing by Chizu Nomiyama, David Gregorio and Diane Craft)

data.title: Australian pension fund UniSuper says local currency undervalued, increases hedging
data.description:
SYDNEY, Feb 20 (Reuters) - One of Australia's largest pension funds, UniSuper, has increased hedging of its overseas investments as it says the local currency is undervalued and will rise as the interest rate differential between Australia and the U.S. widens.

The ‚ÄãAussie dollar was trading at $0.7056 on Friday, down from ‚Äåthis month's three-year top of $0.71465 but well up from the low of $0.6422 in November, when the currency started its climb.

The ‚ÅÝAussie's strength has prompted a number of Australian pension funds, known locally as superannuation funds, ‚ÅÝto increase their hedging of international investments to reduce exposure to ‚Äåthe U.S. dollar.

John ‚ÄåPearce, the chief investment officer of UniSuper, which has A$166 billion ($116.8 billion) in funds under management, said the fund had "tweaked" ‚Äãits hedging strategy lately as it believes the ‚Äålocal currency should be trading higher.

"I've been surprised that it's taken the Aussie so long to get to 70 cents to be honest," Pearce ‚Äãtold Reuters in an interview.

"Commodity prices are pretty ‚Äãstrong. Usually ‚Äåwhen your terms of trade are strong, the Aussie would trade higher than it is," he said.

"It's really been the interest rate differential that's been keeping it ‚ÅÝlower. And now that's getting in the Aussie's favour, I think you're going ‚ÅÝto see upward pressure."

The Aussie strengthened more than 1% earlier this month when the Reserve Bank of Australia raised the cash rate by a quarter point to 3.85%. Financial markets predict at least one more hike this year.

Meanwhile, the U.S. Federal Reserve held its benchmark rate ‚ÅÝsteady in ‚ÄåJanuary in the 3.50%-3.75% range. Traders price in roughly two rate ‚Äåcuts this year, according to the CME Group's FedWatch Tool.

Australian pension funds invested in U.S. ‚ÅÝequities would typically have very little currency hedging because the U.S. dollar tends to act as a safe haven, appreciating when there are negative shocks.

"We're actually now getting paid to hedge," Pearce said. "When Aussie rates were below U.S. rates, it was costing money. Now there's a carry, that's one positive."

Australian Retirement Trust (ART), the country's second-largest pension fund, and HESTA have both increased hedging of overseas investments in ‚Äãrecent months, Reuters reported.

Pearce downplayed worries that Australian pension funds holding more Aussie dollars could place undue upward pressure on the currency.

"I just don't see a world where that's going to ‚Äãbe a problem," he said, because ‚Äåthe currency market "is just so big."

($1 = 1.4215 Australian dollars)

(Reporting by Scott ‚ÄãMurdoch; Editing by Kevin Buckland)

data.title: US economic growth likely slowed to a still-brisk pace in fourth quarter
data.description:
WASHINGTON, Feb 20 (Reuters) - U.S. economic growth likely slowed to a still-solid pace in the fourth quarter because of disruptions from last year's government shutdown and a moderation in consumer spending, though tax cuts and investment in artificial intelligence were expected to drive activity this year.

The anticipated slowdown in gross domestic product would follow back-to-back quarters of robust growth. The Commerce Department will publish on Friday its advance estimate of fourth-quarter GDP, which was delayed by the record 43-day government shutdown.¬Ý

The report is expected to highlight a ‚Äãjobless economic expansion as well as a "K-shaped" economy, where upper-income households are doing well while lower-income consumers ‚Äåare struggling amid high inflation from import tariffs and stalling wage growth. Those conditions have created what economists and President Donald Trump's opponents call an affordability crisis.

"We'll end the year still on a solid note in terms of growth, but it doesn't ‚ÅÝreally translate to feel as good as it looks on paper to most Americans," said Diane Swonk, chief economist at consulting firm KPMG.¬Ý

GDP LIKELY INCREASED 3.0%: SURVEY

GDP probably increased ‚ÅÝat a 3.0% annualized rate last quarter after accelerating at a 4.4% pace in the July-September quarter, a Reuters survey of economists ‚Äåpredicted. The survey was, however, completed before ‚Äådata on Thursday showing the trade deficit widening to a five-month high in December.

The second straight monthly deterioration in the trade deficit led the Atlanta Federal Reserve to cut its GDP estimate to a 3.0% rate from a 3.6% pace.¬Ý

The ‚Äãnonpartisan Congressional Budget Office estimated the government shutdown would subtract 1.5 percentage points from fourth-quarter GDP ‚Äåthrough fewer services provided by federal workers, lower federal spending on goods and services and a temporary reduction in Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program benefits.

The CBO estimated most of the decline in GDP would eventually be recovered, though between $7 billion and $14 billion would not. Economists estimated the economy grew 2.2% in ‚Äã2025 after expanding 2.8% in 2024. Only 181,000 jobs were added last year, the fewest ‚Äãoutside the pandemic since ‚Äåthe 2009 Great Recession, and down from 1.459 million in 2024.¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý

"You have a confluence of shocks affecting the U.S. economy," said Gregory Daco, chief economist at EY-Parthenon. "You have on the one hand the drag from higher prices, tariffs, trade restrictions and reduced immigration, but also the boost from AI investment and the continued ‚ÅÝstrong momentum in terms of stock prices supporting ongoing spending by the more affluent consumers."¬Ý

GROWTH IN CONSUMER SPENDING LIKELY SLOWED

Growth in consumer spending is expected to have slowed ‚ÅÝfrom the third quarter's brisk 3.5% pace. Economists say spending has largely been driven by higher-income households and has been at the expense of saving as inflation eroded buying power.¬Ý

"Getting richer is one thing, but most households rely on incomes to pay bills, and real disposable income pretty much stalled in the quarter," said Sal Guatieri, a senior economist at BMO Capital Markets.

Consumer spending could get a tailwind from what economists anticipate will be larger tax refunds this year because of tax cuts. A solid pace of business investment is expected, mostly related to AI. ‚ÅÝThe jump in imports ‚Äåin December was partly driven by capital goods, mostly computer accessories and telecommunications equipment amid a data center construction boom to ‚Äåpower AI.¬Ý

That should offset any drag on GDP growth from trade.¬Ý

Economists estimated AI, including data centers, semiconductors, software and research and development, accounted for a third of GDP growth in the first ‚ÅÝthree quarters of 2025, blunting the hit from tariffs and reduced immigration.

"It's a significant contribution from a sector that traditionally has represented a small share of the economy," said EY-Parthenon's Daco. "It's also been a key source of volatility in the trade data, because a lot of what we are building here and creating is imported."¬Ý

Economists estimated that trade made little or no contribution to GDP after helping to boost growth for two straight quarters. Inventories were another wild card, having subtracted from GDP for two consecutive quarters.¬Ý

Residential investment is forecast to have contracted for the fourth quarter in a row as builders and prospective homebuyers struggled with higher borrowing costs.¬Ý

The stale report will probably have no impact on monetary policy. But Federal Reserve officials are likely to keep an eye on December's Personal Consumption Expenditures inflation data, due to ‚Äãbe released at the same time as the GDP report.

Economists polled by Reuters forecast PCE inflation, excluding the volatile food and energy components, rising 0.3%. Core PCE inflation rose 0.2% in November from the previous month. Core PCE inflation was projected to have increased 2.9% year-on-year after rising 2.8% in November. The U.S. central bank has a 2% inflation target.

"The year-on-year growth rate ‚Äãof the core has shown essentially no progress since mid-2024," said Lou Crandall, ‚Äåchief economist at Wrightson ICAP. "Many Fed officials anticipate at least some improvement in the coming months, but they will want to ‚Äãsee that show up in the actual numbers."

(Editing by Rod Nickel)

data.title: Epstein estate agrees to $35 million settlement in victim class action
data.description:
Feb 19 (Reuters) - Jeffrey Epstein's estate has agreed to pay as much as $35 million to resolve a class action lawsuit that accused two of the disgraced financier's advisers of aiding and ‚Äãabetting his sex trafficking of young women ‚Äåand teenage girls, according to a court filing on Thursday.

Boies Schiller Flexner, a law firm representing ‚ÅÝEpstein victims, announced the settlement in a brief filed in federal ‚ÅÝcourt in Manhattan.

The deal, if approved by a ‚Äåjudge, would bring an ‚Äåend to a 2024 lawsuit filed against Epstein's former personal lawyer Darren Indyke and former ‚Äãaccountant Richard Kahn, who are co-executors ‚Äåof Epstein's estate.

Epstein's estate previously set up a restitution fund that paid out $121 million to victims. The estate also ‚Äãpaid out $49 million in additional settlements ‚Äãto victims.

Neither ‚ÄåIndyke nor Kahn "made any admission or concession of misconduct" as part of the settlement made public on Thursday, their lawyer Daniel ‚ÅÝH. Weiner said in an emailed statement.

"Because they did nothing wrong, ‚ÅÝthe co-executors were prepared to fight the claims against them through to trial, but agreed to mediate and settle this lawsuit in order to achieve finality as to any potential claims against the Epstein Estate," ‚ÅÝWeiner ‚Äåsaid.

Weiner said the settlement would provide "a confidential avenue for ‚Äåfinancial relief" for Epstein victims who have not already resolved claims against ‚ÅÝthe estate.

Epstein died in a New York jail in August 2019. His death was ruled a suicide.

In the 2024 lawsuit, lawyers at Boies Schiller Flexner said Indyke and Kahn helped Epstein create a complex web of corporations and bank accounts that let him hide his abuses and pay victims and recruiters, while ‚Äãleaving them "richly compensated" for their work.

The Boies law firm previously helped obtain $365 million of settlements with JPMorgan Chase ( JPM ) and Deutsche Bank after accusing ‚Äãthem of missing red flags about ‚ÄåEpstein, once a lucrative client.

(Reporting by Jan Wolfe; ‚ÄãEditing by Raju Gopalakrishnan)

data.title: Indonesia secures 19% tariff in US reciprocal trade deal
data.description:
By Stefanno Sulaiman and Stanley Widianto

JAKARTA, Feb 20 (Reuters) - Indonesia and the United States have signed an agreement on reciprocal trade, with Washington maintaining the 19% tariff it had previously agreed for goods exported by the Southeast Asian country, Indonesia said on Friday.

The signing ‚Äãof the agreement by Indonesia's senior economic minister Airlangga Hartarto ‚Äåand U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer followed months of negotiations, after Washington last year agreed to lower Indonesia's exports to the ‚ÅÝUnited States to 19%, down from an initial rate of 32%.

"This deal respects the sovereignty ‚ÅÝof both countries," Airlangga said from Washington during an online press ‚Äåconference with Indonesian media, ‚Äådescribing the deal as a "win-win" for both countries.

Indonesian coffee, chocolate, natural rubber and spices would be tariff-free, the Jakarta government said ‚Äãin a statement, adding it may also get ‚Äåexemptions for nearly 1,700 other commodities, including its top export product palm oil.

Textile products from Indonesia will be subject to a 0% levy under a "Tariff-Rate Quota" ‚Äãmechanism that will be discussed after the signing, ‚ÄãAirlangga said.

During ‚Äåthe negotiations, the United States had agreed to drop its requests to add non-economic provisions to the deal, including those related to nuclear reactor development and the South ‚ÅÝChina Sea, Airlangga said.

The deal did not regulate trade that is categorised as transshipments ‚ÅÝfrom China because there is no such business conducted in Indonesia, he said.

Indonesia will remove tariff barriers on most U.S. products across all sectors and address a range of non-tariff barriers such as local content requirements, according to a White House fact sheet about the agreement. It will ‚ÅÝalso accept ‚ÄåU.S. product standards on vehicle safety, emissions, medical devices and pharmaceuticals.

Indonesia will ‚Äåalso allow and facilitate U.S. investment in critical minerals and energy resources under conditions that are ‚ÅÝsimilar to Jakarta's treatment of its own investors, the USTR said.

The deal takes effect 90 days after both sides complete the related legal procedures, but changes could still occur if both sides agree, Airlangga said.

President Prabowo Subianto has travelled to Washington for the deal and to attend the first leaders' meeting of U.S. President Donald Trump's Board of Peace.

Prabowo and Trump on Friday signed a document titled "Implementation of the Agreement ‚ÄãToward a NEW GOLDEN AGE for the U.S.-Indonesian Alliance" which the White House said "will help both countries to strengthen economic security, promote economic growth, and thereby continuously lead to global prosperity".

Earlier this ‚Äãweek, Indonesian and U.S. companies had signed ‚Äådeals worth $38.4 billion.

(Reporting by Stefanno Sulaiman, Stanley Widianto, Gayatri Suroyo; Editing ‚Äãby John Mair and David Stanway)

data.title: Fed policy is in a good place, Daly says

Feb 19 (Reuters) - San Francisco Federal Reserve President Mary Daly on Thursday said ‚Äãthat with the labor ‚Äåmarket in a better place because ‚ÅÝof the Fed's rate cuts ‚ÅÝlast year, and ‚Äåwith inflation ‚Äåexpected to resume its decline as ‚Äãthe impact ‚Äåof tariffs rolls off, monetary policy is where ‚Äãit should be.

data.title: Best Payroll Software UK (Feb 2026): QuickBooks Named Best Payroll Solution for UK Small Businesses by Better Business Advice
data.description:
NEW YORK, Feb. 19, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Better Business Advice has recognised Intuit's QuickBooks Payroll as one of the best payroll software solutions available to UK businesses. The acknowledgment shows how organisations are prioritising platforms that integrate compliance, automation and financial processes.

Best Payroll Software

  • QuickBooks - a cloud-based accounting and payroll platform designed to help UK businesses manage finances, compliance and day to day payroll tasks in one system

Payroll administration remains a significant operational demand for UK employers of all sizes. Complexity around statutory requirements, pension auto-enrolment, and real-time information (RTI) submissions to HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC) has driven demand for software that can reduce administrative burden while preserving accuracy and compliance. QuickBooks has been known for delivering a scalable option for these needs.

Better Business Advice's assessment focuses on current feature sets, the depth of payroll integration, user accessibility and compliance support. QuickBooks Payroll functionality is one of the strong choices for UK businesses who are looking for cloud-based payroll combined with comprehensive accounting operations and financial reporting.

Integrated Payroll With Core and Advanced Tiers

QuickBooks Payroll is offered in tiered solutions designed to reflect varying business needs. The Core payroll plan is suited for micro-businesses and small employers who require straightforward payroll processing coupled with essential compliance support. Features include the ability to submit payroll data in real time to HMRC, manage weekly or monthly pay periods, calculate statutory payments and handle pension assessments and submissions. Core payroll emphasises simplicity and ease of use for employers that prioritise essential payroll tasks.

For employers with more complex requirements, QuickBooks Advanced payroll expands on this foundation with additional capabilities. Its extended feature set includes more powerful HR-related functions such as timesheets, leave management and rotas that can be used to inform payroll runs. It also supports enhanced reporting with over 30 predefined reports, custom report creation, and scheduled automatic delivery. Wider statutory payment handling and flexible pay schedules aim to support mid-sized employers and those with multifaceted workforce structures.

Across both tiers, the payroll system integrates directly with QuickBooks Online accounting software, meaning businesses have a unified platform for financial and payroll data. This integration reduces duplication of effort and helps maintain consistency between payroll outcomes and broader financial records.

Regulatory and Operational Overview

The UK payroll environment continues to change, with increasing expectations around timely and accurate submission of payroll data to HMRC and seamless pension enrolment and reporting. Solutions that offer cloud-based access allow employers and finance teams to manage payroll remotely, which has become increasingly valued in hybrid working contexts.

By combining payroll processing with core accounting tools, QuickBooks supports organisations in managing payroll alongside invoicing, expense tracking and financial reporting. This can be particularly beneficial for small businesses that may otherwise rely on disparate tools or manual processes. Services that help businesses migrate from existing payroll providers further support transitions into cloud payroll.

Pricing and Accessibility

QuickBooks Payroll is offered as an add-on to QuickBooks Online accounting plans. Industry pricing guides identify the Core and Advanced payroll plans at accessible entry points for SMEs, with additional per-employee fees that scale with business size. This tiered approach lets organisations align payroll costs with their operational scale, although pricing may vary based on specific accounting plan choices and promotional offers.

As payroll software options proliferate, business leaders are weighing cost, integration potential and compliance assistance when selecting solutions. QuickBooks's combination of payroll and accounting functions responded to such criteria in Better Business Advice's evaluation.

What This Means for UK Employers

The acknowledgement of QuickBooks Payroll signals ongoing changes in how organisations handle payroll processes. Integrated cloud platforms that offer automation, compliance support, and simplified reporting help reduce the administrative load on finance teams. For SMEs without dedicated payroll staff, tools that streamline statutory payroll obligations and connect directly to accounting data can free resources for core business growth.

Better Business Advice's recognition of QuickBooks as a top payroll solution signals the relevance of unified financial systems in a competitive market, where adaptability and compliance remain key priorities for UK employers.

data.title: METALS-LME copper set for third weekly decline on growing inventories, low liquidity
data.description:
Feb 20 (Reuters) - LME copper eased on Friday, set for a third week of decline, with the markets in top consumer China on the Lunar New Year break with rising inventories and a firmer U.S. dollar weighing on the red metal.
    Benchmark ‚Äãcopper on the LME was down 0.1% at $12,793 ‚Äåa metric ton as of 0351 GMT and was down 0.7% for the week.
     The Shanghai Futures Exchange will reopen ‚ÅÝon February 24.

    Industrial metals are retreating as demand softens and supply dislocation is set ‚ÅÝto normalise, analysts at ANZ noted, adding that copper inventories are ‚Äåreplenishing globally.
    Copper stocks ‚Äåin LME-approved warehouses increased by 925 tons to 225,575 tons on Thursday , the highest since March 2025.
Meanwhile, CME Group ( CME ) ‚Äãhas received an application to list a copper ‚Äåwarehouse in a suburb of Chicago, a notice from the U.S. commodity exchange operator shows, as the prospect of import tariffs has led ‚Äãtraders to stockpile.
    "We see key support on ‚Äã3-month copper ‚Äåat two levels - $12,528/ton and 12,414/ton - the latter being the more critical level that needs to hold," said Marex analyst Edward Meir in a note.
    On Friday, the ‚ÅÝdollar was poised to cap its strongest weekly performance since October, buoyed by ‚ÅÝa run of better-than-expected economic data, a more hawkish Federal Reserve outlook, and tensions between the U.S. and Iran kept markets on edge.
    A stronger dollar makes greenback-priced metals more expensive for holders of other currencies.
    In other metals, zinc prices ticked up 0.1% to $3,342.50, and ‚ÅÝnickel was ‚Äåsteady at $17,280 a ton.
    Aluminium rose 0.4% to $3,079, while lead lost ‚Äå0.2% to $1,951.
    "Mine disruptions in Chile and Peru, and growing signs that new projects won't come ‚ÅÝonline fast enough to meet demand, are propping up base metals," KCM Trade chief market analyst Tim Waterer said, adding that low inventories and mine closures, delays underpinned prices for nickel and zinc.

data.title: Research Alert: CFRA Keeps Hold Opinion On Shares Of Ebay Inc.
data.description:
01:50 PM EST, 02/19/2026 (MT Newswires) -- CFRA, an independent research provider, has provided MT Newswires with the following research alert. Analysts at CFRA have summarized their opinion as follows:

We keep our $93 target, about 15.5x our 2026 EPS of $6.01 (raised from $5.83), with our 2027 EPS estimate set at $6.39. This multiple sits modestly above EBAY's ( EBAY ) long-term average of ~14x, which we think is warranted, given strong GMV growth over the past several quarters. That said, we have some concerns around the durability of this growth, as it has benefited from near-term tailwinds, such as a recent spike in bullion and collectible coin sales and renewed momentum in Pokemon-related categories. Still, core categories (fashion, collectibles, and parts/accessories) continue to perform well. Looking ahead, we see additional support from the pending acquisition of Depop, the expansion of eBay Live, and the partnership with Facebook Marketplace, all of which should help drive engagement in focus categories. However, we keep our rating at Hold, as we see elevated reinvestment and the lapping of some near-term tailwinds (e.g., marketing efficiencies) weighing on overall earnings growth.

data.title: Nvidia Pivots To $30 Billion Direct Stake In OpenAI, Decoupling From $100B Milestone Plan As Sam Altman Hopes To Remain 'Gigantic Customer'
data.description:
Nvidia Corp. ( NVDA ) has entered the final stages of a landmark $30 billion equity investment in OpenAI, effectively replacing a massive $100 billion infrastructure agreement that had been the subject of intense market speculation.

A Strategic Decoupling

The $30 billion deal, confirmed by sources close to the negotiations, marks a significant pivot from the “non-binding” letter of intent signed in September 2025.

That original plan contemplated a $100 billion investment tied to the deployment of 10 gigawatts of computing power.

Instead, the new $30 billion commitment is a direct stake in OpenAI's current funding round, which is being raised at a $730 billion pre-money valuation.

Crucially, CNBC noted that this investment is “not tied to any deployment milestones,” granting both companies more flexibility as the AI landscape shifts.

Altman Shrugs Off ‘Insanity’

Speaking from the AI Impact Summit in New Delhi, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman addressed the recent volatility in reports regarding the Nvidia ( NVDA ) partnership. Altman sought to¬Ýcalm investor anxiety, calling the swirling rumors of a rift¬Ý“insanity.”

“We love working with Nvidia,” Altman said, emphasizing that OpenAI expects to remain a “gigantic customer” for the long term.

Despite reports of OpenAI exploring internal chip designs, Altman clarified that the company will continue to utilize a “rich portfolio” of hardware, including Nvidia's ( NVDA ) upcoming Vera Rubin platform.

The ‘Too Big To Fail’ Concern

The massive scale of the round‚Äîwhich could top $100 billion with contributions from Amazon.com Inc. ( AMZN ) and SoftBank‚Äîhas raised questions about circular financing, where chipmakers invest in the very startups that buy their hardware.

Altman pushed back on these concerns, stating that “revenue is growing so fast” across the ecosystem that the investments are supported by real-world demand rather than artificial loops. OpenAI's revenue reportedly surged past $20 billion in 2025, providing a buffer for its high annual burn rate.

“The energy here and what people are building is remarkable,” Altman told CNBC, referring to the global AI buildout. “We’re in the beginning of the largest infrastructure buildout in history.”

Market Impact

Nvidia’s shift to a direct equity stake is seen as a validation moment for tech stocks.

By decoupling from the 10-gigawatt milestone plan, Nvidia ( NVDA ) secures a significant ownership position in the AI leader while avoiding the rigid capital commitments that had previously concerned internal executives and regulators alike.

"With a three-day weekend coming ‚ÅÝup (in Japan), there's a move to lock ‚Äåin profits for ‚Äånow on concern that something might happen between the U.S. and Iran ‚Äãduring the holiday," said Yutaka ‚ÄåMiura, senior technical analyst at Mizuho Securities. U.S. President Donald Trump warned Iran it must ‚Äãmake a deal over its ‚Äãnuclear ‚Äåprogram or "really bad things" will happen, and set a deadline of 10 to 15 days, drawing a ‚ÅÝthreat from Tehran to retaliate against U.S. bases in ‚ÅÝthe region if attacked.

The air transport sector was among the worst performers among the Tokyo Stock Exchange's 33 industry groups with a 3.1% drop. Japanese air ‚ÅÝcarriers ‚ÄåANA Holdings lost 3.2% and Japan Airlines ( JPNRF ) shaved ‚Äå3%.

data.title: Analysis-Toyota's Sato was the man for the moment - then the moment changed
data.description:
By Maki Shiraki and Norihiko Shirouzu

TOKYO, Feb 20 (Reuters) - When Koji Sato was named Toyota ( TM ) CEO in 2023, he appeared to be the man for the moment: a veteran engineer who could give the world's best-selling automaker a badly needed boost in electric vehicles. But after just three years in the top job, he is being replaced by Chief Financial Officer Kenta Kon, a close ally and former secretary of Chairman Akio Toyoda.

The management change, announced this month, will see Sato become Toyota's ( TM ) vice chairman and ‚Äãchief industry officer from April. He delivered record sales and profit and, according to two people familiar ‚Äåwith the matter, is regarded by suppliers and investors as a sharp and talented leader. Yet he will have had one of the shortest tenures on record for a Toyota ( TM ) boss.¬Ý

In recent months he was absent from some high-profile events that ‚ÅÝToyoda attended, leading to speculation among executives about his future, according to three people. Two of the sources said there was no evidence of any falling-out between the men. ‚ÅÝThe people declined to be identified because of the sensitivity of the issue.

Instead, the shake-up reflected Toyoda's thinking that his hand-picked ‚Äåsuccessor was no longer the best fit for ‚Äåthe job given mounting cost pressures, three people said. The grandson of Toyota's ( TM ) founder, Toyoda was CEO for almost 14 years prior to Sato.¬Ý

This account contrasts with the explanation given by Sato at a press conference announcing the change ‚Äãand includes previously unreported details. In an on-stage interview with Toyota's ( TM ) own media outlet, Sato said ‚ÄåToyoda was not involved in the decision.¬Ý

"Toyota ( TM ) keeps emphasising, over and over, that Akio Toyoda wasn't involved in the personnel decision," said Seiji Sugiura, a senior analyst at Tokai Tokyo Intelligence Laboratory. "Mr. Sato also says the same thing very carefully - which means he probably was involved."

In a statement to ‚ÄãReuters, Toyota ( TM ) said that Toyoda was not a member of the group that determined executive ‚Äãappointments and not involved ‚Äåin the decision-making. That group had been discussing succession since last year and talks became more concrete when Sato's appointment as chairman of Japan's auto lobby was finalised in late 2025, it said.

After discussing with the group, Sato decided to step down and the proposal was then submitted to the board, Toyota ( TM ) ‚ÅÝsaid. Sato contributed to strengthening Toyota's ( TM ) finances and would be taking on multiple roles to address industry-wide challenges, it said.

TOYOTA FOCUSED ON BREAK-EVEN POINT

The leadership change came as ‚ÅÝthe Japanese automaker was increasingly focused on tackling higher costs, partially due to U.S. tariffs, the three people said. Meanwhile, it needed to invest heavily in technology, two of them said. Although EVs were seen as a less immediate threat given cooling demand, Toyota ( TM ) executives were concerned that it was falling behind global rivals in software development, one of them said.¬Ý

Kon, the incoming CEO, served as Toyoda's secretary for eight years and is known as the architect of a planned buyout of forklift maker Toyota Industries ( TYIDF ). That deal, which would tighten the Toyoda family's ‚ÅÝgrip on a key ‚Äåsupplier, has drawn opposition from minority investors who say it lacks transparency and is significantly underpriced.

While automakers have been hit with ‚Äåbillions of dollars in added expenses since Trump's tariffs took effect in April, Toyota ( TM ) has an additional burden, having pledged to take on higher costs faced by its suppliers. In recent ‚ÅÝyears the automaker has focused more on its "break-even" point - the number of cars it needs to sell to cover its cost. Toyota ( TM ) sees lowering the break-even as an important gauge of management's ability, one person said.

"Over the past year or so, they've been talking a lot about needing to lower the break-even point," said Sugiura, adding that could spell more cost-cutting under Kon.

Toyota ( TM ) doesn't make the break-even point public. It has a legendary reputation for squeezing costs through its philosophy of "kaizen", or continuous improvement, that identifies seven types of waste, including overproduction and defects.

In its statement, Toyota ( TM ) referred to comments by Kon at the same press conference, where he said Toyota ( TM ) must be "vigilant" to withstand even the most challenging external conditions. As CFO, Kon had been at the forefront of efforts to improve earnings, it said.

Kon is ‚Äãalso CFO of Toyota's ( TM ) technology subsidiary, Woven by Toyota ( TM ), where Toyoda's son, Daisuke, is a senior vice president.

Toyota ( TM ) said it expected to spend 360 billion yen ($2.3 billion) in the current financial year to help suppliers. It did not see the outlay as just a cost, it said, but an investment in improving competitiveness.

Toyota ( TM ) this month raised its full-year profit outlook by 12%, ‚Äãhelped in part by cost-cutting. It has fared better than other automakers, thanks to ‚Äåits contrarian bet on gasoline-electric hybrids that now looks prescient.

($1 = 153.6700 yen)

(Reporting by Maki Shiraki and Norihiko Shirouzu; Additional reporting by ‚ÄãDaniel Leussink; Writing by David Dolan; Editing by Stephen Coates)

data.title: West Virginia sues Apple, saying iCloud distributed 'child porn'
data.description:
Feb 19 (Reuters) - West Virginia's attorney general sued Apple ( AAPL ) on Thursday, accusing the iPhone maker of allowing its iCloud service to become what the company's own internal communications called the "greatest platform for distributing child porn."

Attorney General JB McCuskey, a Republican, accused Apple ( AAPL ) of prioritizing user privacy over child safety. His office called the case the first of its kind by a government agency over the distribution of child sexual abuse material on Apple's ( AAPL ) data storage platform.

"These images are a permanent record of a child's trauma, ‚Äãand that child is revictimized every time the material is shared or viewed," McCuskey said in the ‚Äåstatement.

Apple ( AAPL ) in a statement said it has implemented features that prevent children from uploading or receiving nude images and was "innovating every day to combat ever-evolving threats and maintain the safest, most trusted platform for kids."

"All of our industry-leading parental controls and features, like Communication ‚ÅÝSafety - which automatically intervenes on kids' devices when nudity is detected in Messages, shared Photos, AirDrop and even live FaceTime calls - are designed with the safety, security, and privacy of ‚ÅÝour users at their core," Apple ( AAPL ) said.

Apple ( AAPL ) on Thursday said it plans to roll out a feature in the coming weeks that ‚Äåallows users in the U.S. to flag inappropriate¬Ýcontent ‚Äåsuch as nudity directly to Apple ( AAPL ) via a "Report to Apple ( AAPL )" feature. This is already available¬Ýin Australia and the United Kingdom. Apple ( AAPL ) said the expansion was previously planned and not in response to West Virginia's lawsuit.

The U.S. has seen a growing national ‚Äãreckoning over how smartphones and social media harm children. So far, the wave of litigation and public ‚Äåpressure has mostly targeted companies like Meta, Snap, and Google's YouTube, with Apple ( AAPL ) largely insulated from scrutiny.

END-TO-END ENCRYPTION

West Virginia's lawsuit focuses on Apple's ( AAPL ) move toward end-to-end encryption, putting digital files outside the reach of both Apple ( AAPL ) and law enforcement officials. The state alleges Apple's ( AAPL ) use of such technology has allowed child ‚Äãabuse material to proliferate on its platform.

For decades, technology and privacy advocates have sparred over ‚Äãend-to-end encryption. Advocates call this ‚Äåvital to ensuring privacy and preventing widespread digital eavesdropping. Governments insist it hinders criminal investigations.

Apple ( AAPL ) has considered scanning images but abandoned the approach after concerns about user privacy and safety, including worries that it could be exploited by governments looking for other material for censorship or arrest, Reuters has reported.

McCuskey's office cited a text message ‚ÅÝconversation between two employees in which Apple's ( AAPL ) then anti-fraud chief said that because of Apple's ( AAPL ) privacy priorities, it was "the greatest platform for distributing child porn."

The lawsuit in Mason ‚ÅÝCounty Circuit Court seeks statutory and punitive damages and requests that a judge force Apple ( AAPL ) to implement safer product designs including effective measures to detect abusive material.

Alphabet's Google, Microsoft ( MSFT ) and other platform providers check uploaded photos or emailed attachments against a database of identifiers of known child sex abuse material provided by the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children and other clearinghouses.

Until 2022, Apple ( AAPL ) took a different approach. It did not scan all files uploaded to its iCloud storage offerings, and the data was¬Ýnot end-to-end encrypted, meaning law enforcement officials could access it with a warrant.

Reuters in 2020 ‚ÅÝreported that Apple ( AAPL ) planned ‚Äåend-to-end encryption for iCloud, which would have put data into a form unusable by law enforcement officials. It abandoned the plan after ‚Äåthe FBI complained it would harm investigations.

NEURALHASH

In August 2021, Apple ( AAPL ) announced NeuralHash, which it designed to balance the detection of child abuse material with privacy by scanning images¬Ýon users' devices before upload.

Security researchers ‚ÅÝcriticized the system, worrying it could yield false reports of abuse material. This sparked a backlash from privacy advocates who claimed it could be expanded to permit government surveillance.

A month later, Apple ( AAPL ) delayed introduction of NeuralHash, then canceled it in December 2022, the state said in its lawsuit. That same month, Apple ( AAPL ) launched an option for end-to-end encryption for iCloud data called Advanced Data Protection.

"This is a clear attempt to put pressure on Apple ( AAPL ) not just to disable Advanced Data Protection but to mandate that Apple ( AAPL ) search the private files of every iCloud user," said Thorin Klosowski, a security and privacy activist at the Electronic Frontier Foundation.

West Virginia argues that Apple's ( AAPL ) design choices have allowed illegal content to persist and evade law enforcement, causing widespread harm to West Virginia's public health and child-protection systems.

"Apple ( AAPL ) refuses to implement industry-standard tools that would stop the ‚Äãspread of these illegal images, making them an extreme outlier among major tech companies who routinely remove and report this horrific content," Sarah Gardner, CEO of Heat Initiative, a nonprofit group that has criticized Apple ( AAPL ) and other tech firms over their role in child abuse material, said in a statement.¬Ý

While Apple ( AAPL ) did not go through with the effort to scan images being uploaded to iCloud, ‚Äãit did implement a feature called Communication Safety that blurs nudity and other sensitive ‚Äåcontent being sent to or from a child's device.

(Reporting by Nate Raymond in Boston and Stephen Nellis in San Francisco; Editing by ‚ÄãAlexia Garamfalvi, Christopher Cushing, Franklin Paul, David Gregorio and Diane Craft)

data.title: Stocks dip and oil climbs as Trump ramps up Iran threats
data.description:
SINGAPORE, Feb 20 (Reuters) - Stocks fell in Asia on Friday, the dollar headed for its largest weekly gain in four months and oil prices were rising as a U.S. military build up in the Middle East and a rout in private equity stocks sent ripples of nerves across markets.

Japan's Nikkei ‚Äãdropped 1%. Hong Kong's Hang Seng slipped 0.3% on return from ‚Äåthe Lunar New Year break.

Overnight on Wall Street listed private equity stocks took a beating after one manager, Blue Owl, sold assets and permanently stopped ‚ÅÝquarterly redemptions from one of its funds, stoking wider concerns about valuations and liquidity in the sector.

Blue Owl ‚ÅÝstock finished about 6% lower and shares in larger rivals Apollo Global Management ‚Äåand Blackstone fell more than ‚Äå5%. [.N]

Benchmark Brent crude futures touched 6-1/2 month peaks above $72 a barrel as U.S. President Donald Trump set a deadline of 10 to 15 days ‚Äãfor Iran to make a deal over its nuclear program ‚Äåor "really bad things" will happen. [O/R]

Taken together, the news had investors shying away from risk, said Kenji Abe, chief strategist at Daiwa Securities in Tokyo, and positioning ahead of an earnings ‚Äãreport from the world's most valuable company, Nvidia, next ‚Äãweek.

The chipmaker is ‚Äåclose to finalising a $30 billion investment into OpenAI that will replace the long-term $100 billion commitment agreed upon by the companies last year, the Financial Times reported on Thursday, citing sources.

Walmart shares fell about ‚ÅÝ1.4% after new CEO John Furner offered a cautious outlook on the U.S. consumer.

The U.S. trade deficit ‚ÅÝwidened sharply in December, data showed and the goods shortfall in 2025 was the highest on record suggesting Trump's tariffs have had little impact.

DOLLAR NOTCHES WEEKLY GAIN

In foreign exchange trade, the dollar was headed for its largest weekly rise in four months thanks to a patchwork of slightly stronger U.S. data and minutes from the Federal Reserve ‚ÅÝthat suggested ‚Äåno hurry to lower interest rates. [FRX/]

For the week the dollar is up about ‚Äå0.9% on the euro, pushing the common currency to $1.1762. Morning moves were modest on Friday.

Japan's yen was lower after ‚ÅÝdata showed Japanese core inflation at 2% in January, its slowest pace in two years, possibly complicating the central bank's hiking path.

For the week, the dollar is up 1.6% to 155.2 yen.

The Australian dollar was holding its own at $0.7047 as a widening yield premium provided a buffer, while the kiwi was hamstrung by the fading chance of early rate hikes and headed for its largest weekly drop of 2026 so far. [AUD/]

U.S. Treasuries were steady, with 10-year yields at 4.06%, while division evident in ‚Äãthe Fed minutes over whether or how fast to cut rates has lifted two-year yields by five basis points over the week to 3.46%. [US/]

"There does not seem to be much point in adding risk ahead of this weekend's ‚Äãuncertainty surrounding the Middle East," saud Spectra Markets' President ‚ÄåBrent Donnelly.

"Today feels like a good day to stay out of trouble."

(Reporting by ‚ÄãTom Westbrook; Editing by Kim Coghill)

data.title: Short-term JGB yields fall as cooling inflation reduces BOJ rate hike pressure
data.description:
TOKYO, Feb 20 (Reuters) - Short-term Japanese government bonds (JGBs) rose on Friday, sending yields lower, after data showing cooling inflation reduced the urgency for early rate hikes ‚Äãby the central bank.

Long-term JGBs were ‚Äåpoised for a weekly gain as consensus built that Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's ‚ÅÝsweeping electoral victory earlier this month would allow her ‚ÅÝto keep to a pledge of "responsible" stimulus.

The two-year ‚Äåyield, the one ‚Äåmost sensitive to Bank of Japan policy rates, decreased 0.5 basis point (bp) ‚Äãto 1.25%. The five-year yield slid ‚Äå2.5 bps to 1.605%. Yields move inversely to bond prices.

Japan's annual core consumer inflation hit 2.0% ‚Äãin January, marking the slowest ‚Äãpace in ‚Äåtwo years, data showed. That's in line with the BOJ's projection that inflation will briefly slow below its ‚ÅÝ2% target.

Long-term JGB yields surged to record highs ‚ÅÝlast month as concerns about Japan's fiscal health swelled after Takaichi, a fiscal dove, called a snap election and pledged to cut sales taxes on food for two years.

But a ‚ÅÝmeasure ‚Äåof calm has returned to the market ‚Äåin recent weeks, with yields falling and resilient demand seen ‚ÅÝin JGB auctions.

"It may be that Japan's one-party dominance system is attracting global attention amid political instability in major developed nations, thereby stimulating demand from investors who previously showed little interest in JGBs," Ataru Okumura, a senior strategist at SMBC Nikko Securities, said in ‚Äãa report.

The benchmark 10-year JGB yield fell 5 bps to 2.090%. Longer-dated securities had yet to trade but were ‚Äãpoised for steep declines this ‚Äåweek.

(Reporting by Rocky Swift in Tokyo; Editing ‚Äãby Subhranshu Sahu)

data.title: Australia's Santos signs gas supply agreement for Whyalla Steelworks

Santos said it will supply ‚Äãgas from its ‚ÄåCooper Basin assets from 2030 and that the annual ‚Äãcontract quantity represents ‚Äãaround ‚Äå30% of its current gas production from Cooper Basin.

"Santos gas ‚ÅÝwill be used to enable ‚ÅÝWhyalla to deploy direct reduced iron technology that can process local magnetite ore to produce ‚ÅÝlow-carbon ‚Äåiron," Santos MD and ‚ÄåCEO Kevin Gallagher said.

"This will ‚ÅÝkeep jobs in Whyalla and the Cooper Basin, and reduce emissions by approximately 50% compared to the former coal-fired blast furnace operations."

data.title: US Equity Indexes Fall as Trump Signals Timeline on Iran Diplomacy
data.description:
04:57 PM EST, 02/19/2026 (MT Newswires) -- US equity indexes fell on Thursday, as financials and technology spearheaded a broad-based slide amid rising geopolitical risk in the Middle East and crude oil surging to a six-month high.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 0.5% to 49,395.16, the S&P 500 declined 0.3% to 6,861.89, and the Nasdaq Composite retreated 0.5% to 22,682.73. All sectors except energy, utilities, and industrials dropped.

President Donald Trump on Thursday suggested that a decision on Iran could come within the next 10 days, CNN reported. He again called on Tehran to make a deal, threatening "bad things" if it doesn't, the news report said.

"Now is the time for Iran to join us on a path that will complete what we're doing," Trump was cited as saying. "And if they join us, that will be great. If they don't join us, that will be great too, but it will be a very different path. They cannot continue to threaten the stability of the entire region, and they must make a deal."

West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures jumped 2.4% to $66.73 a barrel, the highest since August.

"At the centre of market anxiety sits the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most critical oil transit chokepoints," Ole Hansen, head of commodity strategy at Saxo Bank, said in a note. "Roughly 19-20 million barrels per day of crude and refined products pass through the narrow waterway -- close to one-fifth of global liquids consumption."

The ICE US Dollar Index rose 0.2% to 97.86, the strongest level in about four weeks.

US Treasury yields were mixed-to-little-changed, with the two-year at 3.47%. The 10-year yield slipped one basis point to 4.07%.

In company news, Deere (DE) raised its full-year earnings guidance amid better-than-expected fiscal Q1 results on the back of double-digit sales growth in its construction and small agriculture businesses. Shares surged 12%, among the top gainers on the S&P 500.

Pool (POOL) shares sank more than 14%, among the steepest declines on the S&P 500, after the company's Q4 adjusted earnings and sales missed analysts' expectations.

Insmed's ( INSM ) shares jumped 6.5%, the top gainer on the Nasdaq, after the company reported a stronger-than-expected Q4 revenue.

In economic news, US initial jobless claims fell sequentially to 206,000 in the week ended Feb. 14 from an upwardly revised 229,000, compared with expectations for 225,000 in a Bloomberg-compiled survey. The four-week moving average slid by 1,000 to 219,000.

The Conference Board's measure of leading indicators declined by 0.2% in December, as expected in a Bloomberg-compiled poll and following a 0.3% decrease in November.

"Overall, the LEI signals weaker economic activity at the start of this year," said Justyna Zabinska-La Monica, Senior Manager for Business Cycle Indicators. "The Conference Board projects a slowdown in growth in Q4 2025 and early 2026, with GDP set to expand by 2.1% year-over-year in 2026, from a forecasted 2.2% in 2025."

The National Association of Realtors' pending home sales index fell by 0.8% in January and was down 0.4% from a year earlier. NAR said that demand should begin to pick up in the coming months, but increased supply is needed.

MT Newswires does not provide investment advice. Unauthorized reproduction is strictly prohibited.

data.title: Oil prices rise as Trump puts time limit on Iran stand-off
data.description:
Feb 20 - Oil prices were higher on Friday as concern of conflict between the U.S. and Iran ratcheted up, with Washington saying Tehran will suffer if it does not ‚Äãagree a deal about its nuclear activity within ‚Äåa matter of days.

Brent crude futures rose 21 cents, or 0.3%, to $71.87,¬Ýwhile U.S. West Texas ‚ÅÝIntermediate crude gained 23 cents, or 0.4% to $66.66.

Prices settled at their ‚ÅÝhighest in six months on Thursday after U.S. ‚ÄåPresident Trump said "really ‚Äåbad things" would happen if Iran does not come to an agreement regarding a nuclear ‚Äãprogram it has said is peaceful ‚Äåbut that the U.S. believes is militaristic. Trump set a deadline of 10 to 15 days.

Iran, meanwhile, has planned ‚Äãa joint naval exercise with Russia, ‚Äãa local ‚Äånews agency reported, days after temporarily closing¬Ýthe Strait of Hormuz for military drills.¬Ý

The major oil producer lies opposite the oil-rich Arabian ‚ÅÝPeninsula across the Strait of Hormuz, through which about 20% ‚ÅÝof global oil supply passes. Conflict in the area could limit oil supplies entering the global market and push up prices.¬Ý

Also supporting oil prices were reports of falling crude oil stocks and limited exports in ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äåworld's biggest oil producing and exporting countries.

U.S. crude inventories ‚Äådropped by 9 million barrels, as refining utilisation and exports climbed, ‚ÅÝshowed an Energy Information Administration report on Thursday.

Oil exports from Saudi Arabia, the world's largest oil exporter, fell to 6.988 million barrels per day in December, the lowest since September, showed data from the Joint Organizations Data Initiative.

Elsewhere, Japan's annual core consumer inflation rate hit 2.0% in January, the slowest in ‚Äãtwo years, potentially dragging on any central bank plan to raise its policy interest rate.

Low interest rates in oil-importing countries such as Japan are ‚Äãtypically seen as supportive for ‚Äåcrude prices.

(Reporting by Laila Kearney in New York; ‚ÄãEditing by Christopher Cushing)

data.title: GLOBAL MARKETS-Stocks dip and oil climbs as Trump ramps up Iran threats
data.description:
*

Trump sets 10-15 day deadline for Iran deal

*

Private equity stocks rattled by Blue Owl redemption halt

*

Dollar set for largest weekly rise this year

By Tom Westbrook

SINGAPORE, Feb 20 (Reuters) - Stocks fell in Asia on Friday, the dollar headed for its largest weekly gain in four months and oil prices were rising as a U.S. ‚Äãmilitary build up in the Middle East and a rout in ‚Äåprivate equity stocks sent ripples of nerves across markets.

Japan's Nikkei dropped 1%. Hong Kong's Hang Seng slipped 0.3% on return from the Lunar New Year break.

Overnight on ‚ÅÝWall Street listed private equity stocks took a beating after one manager, Blue Owl, sold assets and permanently stopped ‚ÅÝquarterly redemptions from one of its funds, stoking wider concerns about valuations and liquidity ‚Äåin the sector.

Blue Owl ‚Äåstock finished about 6% lower and shares in larger rivals Apollo Global Management ( APO ) and Blackstone fell more than 5%.

Benchmark Brent crude futures touched 6-1/2 month peaks ‚Äãabove $72 a barrel as U.S. President Donald Trump set a deadline ‚Äåof 10 to 15 days for Iran to make a deal over its nuclear program or "really bad things" will happen.

Taken together, the news had investors shying away from risk, said Kenji ‚ÄãAbe, chief strategist at Daiwa Securities in Tokyo, and positioning ‚Äãahead of an ‚Äåearnings report from the world's most valuable company, Nvidia ( NVDA ), next week.

The chipmaker is close to finalising a $30 billion investment into OpenAI that will replace the long-term $100 billion commitment agreed upon by the companies last year, ‚ÅÝthe Financial Times reported on Thursday, citing sources.

Walmart ( WMT ) shares fell about 1.4% after new CEO John Furner offered ‚ÅÝa cautious outlook on the U.S. consumer.

The U.S. trade deficit widened sharply in December, data showed and the goods shortfall in 2025 was the highest on record suggesting Trump's tariffs have had little impact.

DOLLAR NOTCHES WEEKLY GAIN

In foreign exchange trade, the dollar was headed for its largest weekly rise in four months thanks to a patchwork of slightly stronger ‚ÅÝU.S. data ‚Äåand minutes from the Federal Reserve that suggested no hurry to lower interest rates.

For ‚Äåthe week the dollar is up about 0.9% on the euro, pushing the common currency to $1.1762. Morning moves were modest on ‚ÅÝFriday.

Japan's yen was lower after data showed Japanese core inflation at 2% in January, its slowest pace in two years, possibly complicating the central bank's hiking path.

For the week, the dollar is up 1.6% to 155.2 yen.

The Australian dollar was holding its own at $0.7047 as a widening yield premium provided a buffer, while the kiwi was hamstrung by the fading chance of early rate hikes and headed for its largest weekly drop of 2026 so far.

U.S. Treasuries were steady, with 10-year yields at 4.06%, while division ‚Äãevident in the Fed minutes over whether or how fast to cut rates has lifted two-year yields by five basis points over the week to 3.46%.

"There does not seem to be much point in adding risk ahead of this weekend's ‚Äãuncertainty surrounding the Middle East," saud Spectra Markets' President ‚ÄåBrent Donnelly.

"Today feels like a good day to stay out of trouble." (Reporting by ‚ÄãTom Westbrook; Editing by Kim Coghill)
